1.synthesis: 'Then Moses and the people of Israel sang this song to the Lord, saying, "I will sing to the Lord, for he has triumphed gloriously; the horse and his rider he has thrown into the sea"' (v.1). The song celebrates God as warrior, redeemer, and king in three mounting strophes. The first proclaims the overthrow of Pharaoh's army (vv.1–5); the second celebrates the Lord's incomparable right hand ('Who is like you, O Lord, among the gods? Who is like you, majestic in holiness, awesome in glorious deeds, doing wonders?' vv.6–10); the third (vv.11–18) prophetically anticipates the terror that will fall on surrounding nations and God's planting of his people on his holy mountain. 'The Lord will reign forever and ever' (v.18). Miriam the prophetess leads the women in antiphonal response with timbrel and dance (vv.20–21).
1.historical_context: Gill identifies this as the first song recorded in Scripture and as 'a typical one: Moses the composer was a type of Christ the Redeemer; and Israel that joined him were typical of the spiritual Israel of God redeemed by Christ.' Keil and Delitzsch analyze the three-strophe structure, noting that each strophe 'commences with the praise of Jehovah and ends with a description of the overthrow of the Egyptian host.' Adam Clarke on the military significance: 'Pharaoh's principal nobility accompanied their king, and the overthrow they met had reduced Egypt to the lowest extremity — had the Israelites been intent on plunder or conquest, they might have overrun the whole land.' The 'Who is like you among the gods?' (v.11) is not a concession to polytheism but the standard rhetorical form for asserting incomparability: the gods of Egypt failed, and the God of Israel has triumphed over them. Miriam's antiphonal response (vv.20–21) is the earliest biblical record of female prophetic leadership in Israel's corporate worship.
1.literary_structure: The song is structured as three mounting strophes (Keil): (1) vv.1–5: the drowning of Pharaoh's forces, God as warrior; (2) vv.6–10: the incomparable right hand that shattered the enemy; (3) vv.11–18: the theological climax — God's holiness, his guidance of the redeemed to his holy dwelling, the terror of nations, God's eternal kingship. Verse 18 ('The Lord will reign forever and ever') is the song's theological summit: the victory over Egypt is a demonstration of the eternal reign that will never be challenged again. Charles Ellicott notes that verse 19 is parenthetical — a prose summary added to connect the poem to its historical occasion. Miriam's response in verse 21 repeats only the first verse of the song, functioning as the antiphonal refrain of a choir responding to the lead singer.
1.theological_application: Matthew Henry on 'I will sing': 'Moses and the children of Israel, having received so great a salvation by the power of God, sung this triumphant song of praise. Gratitude and joy are the appropriate responses to God's redeeming acts, and they find their proper voice in singing.' Gill on 'the Lord is a man of war' (v.3): 'He is so styled on account of the war he made upon the Egyptians, and the victory he obtained over them. Not that he is properly a man, but the expression denotes his power and his skill in fighting for his people.' Calvin on 'Who is like you among the gods?': 'The song does not grant reality to other gods but uses the comparative form to assert absolute uniqueness — as if to say that nothing in heaven or earth can be compared to him in power, holiness, or wonder.' The eternal reign proclaimed in verse 18 is the controlling vision of Revelation, which deliberately echoes this song (Revelation 15:3: 'the song of Moses the servant of God and the song of the Lamb').
1.christology: Revelation 15:3 explicitly calls the song of the redeemed in heaven 'the song of Moses the servant of God and the song of the Lamb' — identifying the Exodus song as the type that the final redemption will fulfill and exceed. The warrior God who threw 'the horse and his rider into the sea' is the same God who 'disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, by triumphing over them in him' (Colossians 2:15). The song's progression from the immediate military victory (strophe 1) through the incomparable divine character (strophe 2) to the eternal reign (strophe 3) is the christological movement of the New Testament: from the cross (the drowning of sin's power), through the resurrection (the demonstration of divine omnipotence), to the parousia ('he must reign until he has put all his enemies under his feet,' 1 Corinthians 15:25).

22.synthesis: Moses leads Israel from the Red Sea into the wilderness of Shur; three days without water, and then — water at Marah, but bitter: 'they could not drink the water of Marah because it was bitter' (v.23). The people grumble against Moses: 'What shall we drink?' (v.24). Moses cries to the Lord; the Lord shows him a tree; he throws it into the water and the water becomes sweet. There God makes for Israel 'a statute and a rule' and tests them: 'If you will diligently listen to the voice of the Lord your God, and do that which is right in his eyes, and give ear to his commandments and keep all his statutes, I will put none of the diseases on you that I put on the Egyptians, for I am the Lord, your healer' (v.26). They arrive at Elim — twelve springs and seventy palm trees — and camp by the water (v.27).
22.historical_context: Matthew Henry on the abruptness of the test: 'It should seem it was with some difficulty that Moses prevailed with Israel to leave that triumphant shore on which they sang the foregoing song; but Moses with much ado brought them from the Red Sea into a wilderness.' Adam Clarke on the identity of the tree: its species is unknown; various candidates have been proposed (quassia, whose bitter bark might catalyze chemical neutralization; Oleander; a variety of fig-tree). The Targums of Jonathan and Jerusalem identify it as the ardiphney tree, on which Moses wrote the divine name before casting it in. Adam Clarke draws the cross-connection: 'Many suppose that this tree which healed the bitter waters was symbolical of the cross of our blessed Redeemer, that has been the means of healing infected nature.' The statute of verse 26 is the first conditional covenant clause in the post-Exodus period — 'diligent listening' conditions the promise 'I am the Lord your healer' (Yahweh Rapha), the divine name-as-pledge. Elim with its twelve springs (one per tribe?) and seventy palms is a providentially prepared resting place after the test.
22.literary_structure: The section is a structural microcosm of Israel's entire wilderness experience: crisis (three days without water) → complaint (murmuring against Moses) → prayer (Moses cries to God) → divine provision (tree shown, water sweetened) → covenant statute (obedience conditions healing) → abundant rest (Elim's twelve springs). This sequence — test, complaint, prayer, provision, covenant-instruction, abundance — will repeat throughout the wilderness narratives. The placement immediately after the Song of Moses (vv.1–21) is deliberately contrastive: within hours of the greatest deliverance in Israel's history, the people grumble. The speed of the transition from praise to complaint is the narrative's stark diagnosis of the human heart without sustained faith.
22.theological_application: Matthew Henry on Marah as divine training: 'God brings us into straits that he may bring us to our knees; and perhaps the straits are necessary and the sharper the straits the stronger the cry and the more remarkable the answer.' Gill on the tree: 'by the power of God the water became sweet; not by any natural virtue in the tree, but by the appointment and command of God, as is clear from his showing Moses the tree and directing him to cast it in.' Adam Clarke on the covenant clause of verse 26: 'Moses was not only their leader but also their mediator; of prayer and dependence on the Almighty, the great mass of the Israelites appear to have had little knowledge at this time — Moses therefore had much to bear from their weakness, and the merciful Lord was long-suffering.' The divine self-identification as 'Yahweh Rapha — the Lord your healer' is one of the great covenant names of God: healing is not incidental to his character but definitional.
22.christology: Adam Clarke's typological observation has the fullest patristic and Reformational support: the tree cast into the bitter waters to make them sweet is a figure of the cross of Christ by which the bitterness of suffering, death, and sin is transformed for the believer into the occasion of grace and healing. The cross does not remove bitterness by avoiding it but by entering it — as the tree enters the water. The covenant name 'Yahweh Rapha — the Lord your healer' receives its fullest expression in the ministry of Jesus, who healed as the direct sign of the kingdom's arrival: 'he himself bore our sins in his body on the tree... by his wounds you have been healed' (1 Peter 2:24). Elim's twelve springs — abundance after the test of Marah — anticipate the rest that remains for the people of God (Hebrews 4:9).
